coredata.dc:title: An emerging enol-based 1D covalent organic framework for trace water detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xin
Wu, Ni
Guo, Zhiyong
Zhan, Hongbing
coredata.dc:description: The determination of trace water in organic solvents has been a long-standing research hotspot and is still evolving which requires further development. Ratiometric luminescent sensors with rapid response and accurate visualization of water assay based on porous materials are obviously prospective candidates for trace water detection, but remain a challenge. Herein, we firstly reported a novel one-dimensional (1D) covalent organic framework (COF, labeled as FZU-68) which was built from 2-hydroxyisophthalaldehyde (DIPh) and 1,3,6,8-tetra-(p-aminophenyl)-pyrene (PyTTA). The COF displays comparative high specific surface area, narrow pore size distribution and prominent chemical/thermal stability. Besides, its solid state displays a bright red emission at 606 nm. More impressively, due to a water-induced enol-keto isomerization in its structure, the fluorescence of FZU-68 quenched in response to trace amounts of water, which sustain the limit of detection (LOD) in acetonitrile (MeCN) up to 0.05% (v/v). Besides, FZU-68 could be utilized several times in the above trace water detection experiments. This study not only presents the feasibility of integrating heteroatoms and functional groups into 1D COFs, but also sheds light on the application prospects of COFs in the field of chemical sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 1D COF
Light-emitting
Enol-ketone isomerization
Trace water detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of trace water detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of PyTTA (blue curve), DIPh (green curve) and FZU-68 (red curve). (b) 13C Solid-state NMR spectra of FZU-68. (c) The SEM image of FZU-68 and the magnification is 1 μm. (d) The TEM images of FZU-68. Scale bars are 50 nm and 20 nm (inset).

(a) Experimental PXRD pattern (black curve) and Pawley refined PXRD pattern (red curve) of FZU-68. (b) Difference plot (gray curve) between experimental and Pawley refined patterns. Simulated PXRD patterns for (c) AA-1D structure, (d) AB-1D structure, (e) AA-2D structure, and (f) AB-2D structure. Those structures with their unit cell parameters of AA-1D (g), AB-1D (h), AA-2D (i) and AB-2D (j).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (a) and pore size distribution (b) of the FZU-68.

(a) The solid-state fluorescence emission spectrum of FZU-68 (blue line, λex = 398 nm) and its monomers DIPh (red line, λex = 383 nm) and PyTTA (black line, λex = 398 nm). (b) Fluorescence spectra of FZU-68 with different water contents in anhydrous MeCN (λex = 398 nm) and the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and different water contents (inset).

(a) Chemical structures of the different tautomers mechanism of small-molecule model compound in the presence of water and FZU-68 enol-ketone isomerization diagram after drying and water absorption. (b) FT-IR spectrum of dry and humid FZU-68 and (c) The curve for the FZU-68 recyclability of detecting water in MeCN.
The ESIPT process for the dual emission of FZU-68. The blue emission at 480 nm was normal fluorescence from the enol state, whereas that at 600 nm with a larger Stokes shifts was the ESIPT emission.

head.ce:title: An emerging enol-based 1D covalent organic framework for trace water detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350118, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination of trace water in organic solvents has been a long-standing research hotspot and is still evolving which requires further development. Ratiometric luminescent sensors with rapid response and accurate visualization of water assay based on porous materials are obviously prospective candidates for trace water detection, but remain a challenge. Herein, we firstly reported a novel one-dimensional (1D) covalent organic framework (COF, labeled as FZU-68) which was built from 2-hydroxyisophthalaldehyde (DIPh) and 1,3,6,8-tetra-(p-aminophenyl)-pyrene (PyTTA). The COF displays comparative high specific surface area, narrow pore size distribution and prominent chemical/thermal stability. Besides, its solid state displays a bright red emission at 606 nm. More impressively, due to a water-induced enol-keto isomerization in its structure, the fluorescence of FZU-68 quenched in response to trace amounts of water, which sustain the limit of detection (LOD) in acetonitrile (MeCN) up to 0.05% (v/v). Besides, FZU-68 could be utilized several times in the above trace water detection experiments. This study not only presents the feasibility of integrating heteroatoms and functional groups into 1D COFs, but also sheds light on the application prospects of COFs in the field of chemical sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of trace water has been an indispensable process in plenty of chemical productions due to its vital influence on the yield and process of numerous chemical reactions [1]. Although the traditional analytical techniques for the detection of trace water feature outstanding precision reach to one millionth level, such as Karl Fischer titration and chromatography [2], there are some obstructions, like the toxicity of the reagents used, long analysis time, cumbersome operation, the need for professional instruments, and so on. In recent decades, several luminescent-based sensors possess the benefits of simple operation and low detection cost have been developed, that is, different types of fluorescent probes [3], including luminescent metal nanoclusters [4–6], metal-organic frameworks [7,8], polymers [9,10], and carbon dots [11–13] could be utilized to detect and quantify the content of trace water by monitoring fluorescence intensity. However, most of them are either troublesome to be recovered from water or unstable in water [14,15]. Hence, it is imminent to develop luminescent sensors possess higher sensitivity as well as affordable recyclability.

Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) materials with two-dimensional (2D) layered and three-dimensional (3D) network structures have been successfully explored. Their good stability, porosity, and crystallinity give them broad application prospects in gas storage and separation [16–18], catalysis [19–23], sensor [24,25], optoelectronics [26–28], and energy storage [29–32]. Since the concept of COFs was proposed in 2005, extensive research has been carried out on its topology, construction modules, synthesis methods and application fields in the past several years. However, there are relatively few reports on COFs with one-dimensional (1D) chain structure so far [33–35]. Remarkably, COFs for water detection and adsorption properties have attracted extensive attraction in basic and applied research. However, at present there are rare reports on the utilization of COFs for detection of trace water in organic solvents [36,37]. In 2017, Yan's group [15] employed 4,4′,4″-(1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triyl)trianiline (Tz) and 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalaldehyde (Da) to prepare 2D COF as a ratiometric sensor with a dual effect of excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) and intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) to detect trace water content in organic solvents. In 2020, Tian research group [38] further replaced Da with its analogue (2,5-bis(methylthio)terephthalaldehyde, BMTA) and obtained a similar COF which showed only ICT effect for trace water detection.

Herein, we firstly proposed a novel enol-based 1D COF (termed as FZU-68) that was constructed by solvothermal reaction using 1,3,6,8-tetra-(p-aminophenyl)-pyrene (PyTTA) as D2h symmetry monomer and 2-hydroxyisophthalaldehyde (DIPH) as 120o nonlinear C2 monomer (Scheme 1 ). It was demonstrated that eclipsed packing (AA) of the 1D structure was in best agreement with the experimental PXRD pattern. FZU-68 was further explored as a ratiometric sensor to detect trace water in organic solvents with high sensitivity. Its fluorescence variation mechanism is proposed to be that the breaking of intramolecular hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl and imine bonds stimulated by trace water, enabling enol-keto isomeric transformation. Thus, this study enriches the structural types of 1D COFs and lay a great foundation for exploring chemical sensing in porous materials.

All solvents and reagents were obtained commercially and used without further purification unless noted. Anhydrous MeCN, acetone and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were prepared using a solvent drying system. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer using KBr pellets. The 13C-solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (13C-SS-NMR) spectra of the FZU-68 were undertaken on a Bruker Advance III 500 nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on a Nova NanoSEM 230. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the FZU-68 were obtained in JEM-2100 Plus (LaB6 filament) operated at 200 kV. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on NETZSCH STA449-F5 by heating the samples from 35 to 900 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min and a gas flow rate of 20 mL/min. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were carried out on a Rigaku Miniflex 600 diffractometer using a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15416 nm). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis was carried out at 77 K using Micromeritics ASAP 2020 Plus. Prior to gas sorption, the COF sample (60 mg) was activated by drying at 120 °C under a dynamic vacuum for 12 h. The surface areas were evaluated using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model applied between P/P0 values of 0.05 and 0.3. The fluorescence emission spectrum (PL spectrum) of the FZU-68 was measured by a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer of Edinburgh FLS1000.

A Pyrex tube was charged with DIPh (5.4 mg, 0.036 mmol), PyTTA (10.2 mg, 0.018 mmol), 0.7 mL mesitylene, 0.3 mL dioxane and 0.1 mL 6 M acetic acid (AcOH). The tube with the mixture was placed and sonicated for 20 min and then degassed through three freeze-pump-thaw cycles, which was tightly capped and then heated at 120 °C for 72 h yielding solid at the bottom of the tube before the next isolation procedure. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation and subsequently washed for 24 h by Soxhlet extractions with anhydrous acetone, THF and methanol, respectively. Eventually, the resultant products were dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum overnight to afford 13.4 mg (91% isolated yield) dark red powder.

FZU-68 (1 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL anhydrous MeCN, and ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min to form a fluorescence titration assay. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature while repeated experiments were executed in pursuit of consistent and credible data. Then different volumes of deionized water were added to the suspension to record the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra. It is noteworthy, for all spectral tests, that the COF suspension was excited at λex = 398 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength was monitored from 420 to 700 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was calculated as follows:
(1)
η =
[
(
I 0
− I
)
/
I 0
]
× 100 %
η is the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of COF suspensions without and with the addition of the analyte, respectively.

And the value of the limit of detection (LOD) was determined according to the following equation:
(2)
S b
=
∑
i = 1
n
(
x i
−
x ‾
)
2
n − 1
(3)
k =
Δ l
Δ c
(4)
L O D =
3
S b
k

The standard deviation (Sb) was calculated by measuring the initial intensity of fluorescence of the probe molecules in anhydrous MeCN eleven times. Where Δl is the change in fluorescent intensity and Δc is the change in quencher concentration.

To determine the successful synthesis of the materials, FT-IR and 13C-SS-NMR spectrum were performed for both monomers and the synthesized COF. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the infrared absorption peak at 1624 cm−1 confirms the formation of CN bonds. Additionally, the hydroxyl stretching vibration peak near 2873 cm−1 for monomer DIPh and the weakened strength of N–H bond at 3346 cm−1 for monomer PyTTA imply that a Schiff base reaction transpired successfully between the two monomers. From the 13C-SS-NMR spectra (Fig. 1b), it can be readily discerned that the chemical shift near 157.55 ppm corresponds to the signal peak of carbon in the CN bond, and the other carbon atoms at different positions corresponding to the peaks are shown below, further signifying the successful synthesis of FZU-68. The morphology of FZU-68 was characterized employing scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1c, the SEM photograph of FZU-68 at different magnifications illustrate a needle-like morphology. As shown in Fig. 1d, the TEM image shows that FZU-68 manifests a lamellar π-π packing structure with a chain-to-chain stacking and the detected lattice fringes were at a distance of 1.46 nm (Fig. S1).

In term of topological classification, the combination of D2h symmetry and nonlinear C2 symmetry will foster two possible crystallographic systems: orthogonal crystal systems and hexagonal crystal systems. Considering the complexity of each architecture accumulation, as exhibited in Fig. 2 g–j, four possible crystal structures are assumed here, one-dimensional eclipsed structure (AA-1D), one-dimensional staggered structure (AB-1D), two-dimensional eclipsed structure (AA-2D) and two-dimensional staggered structure (AB-2D). The PXRD pattern of the synthesized FZU-68 was brought into further verify its crystal structure. The initial lattice parameters and fractional atomic coordinates of AA-1D FZU-68 are listed in Tables S1–S2. Meanwhile, the structure was simulated by adopting Materials Studio 8.0 software. The Orthorhombic topology in IMA2 space group is utilized to model the structure, and the unit cell parameters are further refined from PXRD pattern by Pawley fitting. As show in Fig. 2, it can be observed that the peak positions of the experimental PXRD spectra of COF is almost the same as that of the imaged PXRD spectra of AA-1D, which is indeed different from the simulation results of AB-1D, AA-2D and AB-2D. Forcite optimization was proceeded accordance with the one-dimensional Orthorhombic topology of AA-1D stacking mode. The simulated PXRD values are basically alignment with the experimental values, and the (110), (310), (420) and (312) crystal planes of the topology are 5.70o, 8.45o, 13.75o and 24.35o, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, the experimental values were refined, which were a = 40.3904 Å, b = 16.7527 Å, c = 7.7855 Å, α = β = γ = 90o, R wp = 7.65%, R p = 5.56%, respectively. The small error in the Pawley simulation accounts for the fact that the calculated cell parameters are essentially equal to the fitted cell parameters, thus ensuring the reasonableness of the 1D COF structure determined through modeling. The above data reflect that FZU-68 is an orthorhombic topology with AA-1D accumulation, which is very similar to that of our previous report [34]. Moreover, the PXRD spectra of DIPh and PyTTA were also measured and compared with FZU-68, which reveals that the PXRD peak of FZU-68 indeed came from itself rather than the residual building blocks (Fig. S3).

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the nitrogen adsorption isotherm of FZU-68 was measured at 77 K and classified as type I isotherm curve, manifesting its microporous feature. When P/P0 > 0.9, the nitrogen absorption increased significantly with a hysteresis loop, which was induced by the voids formed by the loose accumulation of powder. The specific Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were estimated as 743.7 m2/g. Alternatively, as shown in Fig. 3b, the pore size distribution curve obtained by fitting the adsorption curve with generalized function theory (NLDFT) yields a porosity value of 7.7 Å for the material, which fits the 1D structure after calculations. The above results are indicative of the high specific surface area and narrow pore size distribution of the novel enol-based 1D FZU-68.

Concerning the chemical stability of materials, the PXRD spectrum of FZU-68 soaked in THF, ethyl alcohol (EtOH), hexane, acetone, dimethylformamide (DMF), 1 M HCl and 1 M NaOH were monitored (Fig. S4). The positions and intensities of the peaks of FZU-68 did not change significantly after immersing in the previously described organic solvents for 24 h, which is a convincing proof that the FZU-68 preserves excellent chemical steadiness in both ordinary organic solvents and acid-base aqueous solutions. Simultaneously, its thermal stability was inspected by TGA. The experimental result strongly proves that FZU-68 maintains 96% of its own weight in the presence of N2 even temperature levels up to 450 °C (Fig. S5), leading to a satisfactory thermal stability.

With respect to the optical properties of the materials, the solid-state fluorescence spectrum of DIPh, PyTTA and FZU-68 were collected and evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the emission peaks of PyTTA, DIPh and FZU-68 are located at 520 nm, 512 nm, 606 nm, respectively. The emitting peak of the COF material distinctly derived from its own intrinsic rather than the residual monomers, further implying the successfully synthesis of the target COF material. Intriguingly, when FZU-68 is exposed to water, its orange color changed to dark brown immediately (Fig. S6). This phenomenon prompted us to investigate its capability in the aspect of trace water detection. Anhydrous MeCN, THF and acetone were elected as the organic dispersion solvent. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of FZU-68 suspension located at 480 nm which belongs to the enol groups of FZU-68 trended down proportionally with the increment of water content in anhydrous MeCN. Fluorescence intensity monitoring was also done in anhydrous THF and anhydrous acetone, as shown in Fig. S7. Tables S3–S5 exhibited the association between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the percentage of water in organic solvents (anhydrous acetone, THF and MeCN, respectively). It can be noticed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the COF was 11.23% and 52.91% at 0.2% and 3.0% water content in anhydrous MeCN, respectively, which is demonstrative to the high responsiveness and sensitivity of FZU-68 for the detection and qualification against trace water. The correlation between the declining rate of fluorescence intensity and the adding of water content presents a well-defined linear relationship which is plotted in inset of Fig. 4b. The R2 value of 0.9709 was derived from the linear fit and the K value of 514.7059 was calculated from the slope. The LOD values were identified with respect to equations (2)–(4). The calculated LOD detection limit is as low as 0.05% in MeCN, 0.08% in THF and 0.07% in acetone, signifying that FZU-68 offers sufficient detection capability for trace amounts of water in organic solvents. In addition, its isostructural compound named as COF-J3 [39,40] was synthesized as comparison (Figs. S8–S9). As shown in Fig. S10, trace amounts of water have virtually no effect on the fluorescent intensity, which probably due to the lack of intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed between its structure, let alone to achieve the enol to ketone transition. As displayed in Table S6, FZU-68 featured relatively fast response, low LOD and high accuracy as compared to other previous reported trace water detection materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of trace water detection
ce:section.ce:para: The water-promoted switch between enol-type and keto-type isomers is the motive force underlying the utilization of this novel 1D COF for the detection and quantification of trace water [41]. It is assumed that FZU-68 may exhibit dynamic tautomerism with the participation of water (Fig. 5 a). As shown in Fig. 5b and Figs. S11–12, the transition of FZU-68 between the enol form and keto form isomers were specifically explored by FT-IR and 13C-SS-NMR spectra. The FT-IR spectra of trans-keto FZU-68 presents a wider absorption band in the range of 3060-2850 cm−1 corresponding to a stronger resonance-assisted intramolecular hydrogen bonding, reflecting a larger stretching vibration or proton transfer equilibrium process between the O–H enol and N–H trans-keto formulations. In addition, the peak intensities at 1624 and 1533 cm−1 are attained from the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively, which support the trans-ketone form structure. The 13C-SS-NMR spectrum of wet FZU-68 shows a diminution of the imine peak at ca. 145 ppm and the appearance of a new feature at ca. 140 ppm as compared to dry FZU-68, which supported our hypothesis. The reversible property of the probe was further examined (Fig. 5c). The fluorescence of FZU-68 dispersed in MeCN was quenched when adding 3.0% water. After molecular sieves were added, its fluorescence intensity almost returned to the original level. The data reveals that the recycled experiments could be repeated at least four times without obvious changes in its fluorescence intensity. In addition, the XRD pattern of recycled FZU-68 was performed and found that its crystallinity was retained (Fig. S13).

On absorbing water by FZU-68, a dark brown solid trans-keto FZU-68 was yielded. Based on the high sensitivity of FZU-68 material to water molecules, the mechanisms of inducing reversible reciprocal isomerization were evaluated (Fig. 6 ) according to reported article [37]. With the addition of water molecules, the enol form changed to trans-keto, which breakdown the intramolecular hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl and nitrogen atom. Accordingly, the normal emission diminished. However, the structure of the keto group CO was relatively stable in aqueous solution, so the ESIPT emission almost unchanged. The FZU-68 intercalates and isomerizes into stronger electron acceptors through enol ketone reciprocal isomerization, which facilitate the charge transfer process as well as the band gap reduction process. As shown in Fig. S14, the energy gap (ΔE) between HOMO and LUMO lessen from the enol form to the trans-keto form, where the molecules tend to persist in a more stable state. Consequently, this strategy of low-cost and scalable proportional trace water detection and identification through ingenious structural transformations will lay a robust foundation for future exploration in sensing applications.

In summary, phenolic hydroxyl groups employed as functional groups onto the backbone of COFs were adopted as the building monomers to construct 1D chain COFs materials, named FZU-68, through the Schiff base reaction via a solvothermal method. It was characterized that the COF backbone possesses high crystallinity, narrow pore size distribution (7.7 Å), comparative high BET surface area (743.7 m2/g), eminent fluorescence emission capability, remarkable chemical stability and thermal stability (400 °C). Notably, considering the enol-ketone isomerization caused by trace water, it is convenient, efficient and available for reuse for the quantification and detection of trace water for FZU-68 in organic solvents with low detection limits down to 0.05% in MeCN. According to the fact, this detection limit is relatively low. Although there is still a gap with the detection limit of small molecule detection probes, the structural advantages of COFs can be realized which many small molecules cannot. This work apparently not only furnishes a platform for the architecture of highly crystalline 1D COFs, but also delivers a rational design thread for radiometric sensing applications.

Xin Yuan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Ni Wu: Investigation, Data curation. Zhiyong Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongbing Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2022J01086).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2023.112573.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of moisture by fluorescent OFF-ON sensor in organic solvents and raw food products

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Kumar, R. Kaushik, A. Ghosh, D.A. Jose, Detection of Moisture by Fluorescent OFF-ON Sensor in Organic Solvents and Raw Food Products, Analytical Chemistry, 88 (2016) 11314-11318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafast water sensing and thermal imaging by a metal-organic framework with switchable luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Chen, J.-W. Ye, H.-P. Wang, M. Pan, S.-Y. Yin, Z.-W. Wei, L.-Y. Zhang, K. Wu, Y.-N. Fan, C.-Y. Su, Ultrafast water sensing and thermal imaging by a metal-organic framework with switchable luminescence, Nature Communications, 8 (2017) 15985.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments on fluorescent hybrid nanomaterials for metal ions sensing and bioimaging applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.N. Mehta, M.L. Desai, H. Basu, R. Kumar Singhal, S.K. Kailasa, Recent developments on fluorescent hybrid nanomaterials for metal ions sensing and bioimaging applications: A review, Journal of Molecular Liquids, 333 (2021) 115950.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent nanoswitch based on organic-phase copper nanoclusters for sensitive detection of trace amount of water in organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Huang, W. Liu, H. Feng, Y. Ye, C. Tang, H. Ao, M. Zhao, G. Chen, J. Chen, Z. Qian, Luminescent Nanoswitch Based on Organic-Phase Copper Nanoclusters for Sensitive Detection of Trace Amount of Water in Organic Solvents, Analytical Chemistry, 88 (2016) 7429-7434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bonding-induced emission of silyl-protected copper nanoclusters for luminescence turn-on detection of trace water in organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhao, H. Feng, X. Zhang, H. Ao, Z. Qian, Bonding-induced emission of silyl-protected copper nanoclusters for luminescence turn-on detection of trace water in organic solvents, Analyst, 142 (2017) 4613-4617.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of fluorescent Au nanoclusters-based test papers for recyclable detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.-X. Bian, X.-T. Wu, F. Chai, L. Li, L.-Y. Zhang, T.-T. Wang, C.-G. Wang, Z.-M. Su, Facile preparation of fluorescent Au nanoclusters-based test papers for recyclable detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 241 (2017) 592-600.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission of lanthanide metal-organic frameworks encapsulating carbon-based dots for ratiometric detection of water in organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, J. Cai, Q. Fang, X. You, Y. Chi, Dual-Emission of Lanthanide Metal-Organic Frameworks Encapsulating Carbon-Based Dots for Ratiometric Detection of Water in Organic Solvents, Analytical Chemistry, 88 (2016) 1748-1752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent metal-organic framework for food real-time visual monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, D. Li, Y. Ye, Y. Qiu, J. Liu, L. Huang, B. Liang, B. Chen, A Fluorescent Metal-Organic Framework for Food Real-Time Visual Monitoring, Advanced Materials, 33 (2021) 2008020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanochemical activation of disulfide-based multifunctional polymers for theranostic drug release

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Shi, Q. Song, R. Gostl, A. Herrmann, Mechanochemical activation of disulfide-based multifunctional polymers for theranostic drug release, Chemical Science, 12 (2021) 1668-1674.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activatable polymeric nanoprobe for near-infrared fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging of T lymphocytes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, S. He, W. Chen, Y. Liu, X. Zhang, Q. Miao, K. Pu, Activatable Polymeric Nanoprobe for Near-Infrared Fluorescence and Photoacoustic Imaging of T Lymphocytes, Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 60 (2021) 5921-5927.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace water in organic solvents based on carbon quantum dots with yellow fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wei, H. Li, Y. Yuan, C. Sun, D. Hao, G. Zheng, R. Wang, A sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of trace water in organic solvents based on carbon quantum dots with yellow fluorescence, RSC Advances, 8 (2018) 37028-37034.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped fluorescence carbon dots as multi-mechanism detection for iodide and curcumin in biological and food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Tang, H. Yu, B. Bui, L. Wang, C. Xing, S. Wang, M. Chen, Z. Hu, W. Chen, Nitrogen-doped fluorescence carbon dots as multi-mechanism detection for iodide and curcumin in biological and food samples, Bioactive Materials, 6 (2021) 1541-1554.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yellow-visual fluorescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke for the efficient detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, W.-t. Wu, M.-b. Wu, H.-d. Sun, H. Xie, C. Hu, X.-y. Wu, J.-s. Qiu, Yellow-visual fluorescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke for the efficient detection of Cu2+ ions, New Carbon Materials, 30 (2015) 550-559.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of phosphate in aqueous solution based on near infrared fluorescent silver nanoclusters/metal-organic shell composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Dai, C.-X. Yang, X.-P. Yan, Ratiometric Fluorescent Detection of Phosphate in Aqueous Solution Based on Near Infrared Fluorescent Silver Nanoclusters/Metal-Organic Shell Composite, Analytical Chemistry, 87 (2015) 11455-11459.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-crystallinity covalent organic framework with dual fluorescence emissions and its ratiometric sensing application
